speaker: interviewer
text: Today is April 8, 1996. We're with survivor Hy Abrams. And this is tape number 3. Can you continue, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: So the-- the name of the company, with its cement-- which used to work with cement was-- was the name Freitag. I think they're still in existence in Germany. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] The main kapo, over there, was also a German. And I know, for a fact, he was there, because he was a killer. He killed somebody in Germany. So they took him from jail. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they-- and they made him a kapo in that camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] How did you know that? Did you know that while you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm hm. Yeah. Yeah. One day-- one day, when we came out from that mountain, where we were working, to go back to camp, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there was one person missing. We stood there for three hours. Finally, they-- they got that man out from that-- from the-- from his work there. He asked him-- that kapo asked him where he was. I don't know what the guy told the kapo. That kapo beat him so. He beat him to death, right there, with his shoes. He killed the guy right there. Now, Melk, when we-- they made a special-- a special platform for the people, for the-- for the prisoners. They made that platform near the station, to go there. And also, by the fact that they made a special platform, they shouldn't be-- they shouldn't be together with the civilians. When we used to wait for that train to take us back to camp, there used to be-- used to be an express train with civilians going by there almost every day. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The-- I used to-- I used to have such a envy of those people, what used to go by there, by that civilian train. You used to see when they go by there, they are well-fed and so on. I didn't do anything wrong. I am a slave. And they are so free, because they are Germans. Anyway, we were there in that camp. It-- I got sick in that camp. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know what it was. But you'll excuse me, I had diarrhea. And that was a sign-- usually, that was a sign that the end is near in a camp like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I guess God wanted me, I should live and be able to tell what happened. They took me, over there, in a hospital, in that camp. What they did in the hospital, they didn't give me any medicine or anything like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: The only thing what they gave me, they gave me a piece of toast, in the morning, and a piece of toast at night-- for lunch, and a piece of toast at night, no liquid. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] After a week, I went back to the barrack. Of course, I didn't have my-- my clothes, what I had before. I used to have a pair of good shoes there. So I had to-- I had to-- they gave me a pair of wooden shoes. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] Whoever died in that camp, they used to send them to Mauthausen, because Mauthausen had a crematorium. This camp didn't have the crematorium. But in the end, they built a crematorium in that camp, too. I don't know why. Or it was too many-- too many dead people being sent to-- to Mauthausen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you there when they built between the crematorium?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I was there when they built the crematorium.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who actually built it?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the people from the camp. They built it, right there. Like they build it-- as a matter of fact, they built the crematorium night-- right near the kitchen, not far from the kitchen, right there in that camp. Anyway, when it came-- I think it was-- it was a very bad winter. They used to-- we had to take a shower every-- every two weeks. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We had to go naked, in the winter, from the barrack, to go to the shower [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and back, also. It doesn't make any difference how deep the snow was or how cold it was. And by God-- and for God's name, nobody ever got a cold there from that. I think it was in-- in March, by the end of-- by the end-- by the end of February. The-- the-- probably the Allies found out, for some reason or another, where the-- where that factory is. Where that factory is. They started to bomb that mountain, day and night.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we didn't go to work no more there. One day, by the end of February, they took us, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they took us on a march. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know to what city it was. But it was near the Danube.

speaker: interviewee
text: For a couple days, they took us on a march there, till we came to the river Danube. Down there, was a-- was a-- a boat waiting for us. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In that boat, there was tar. That boat, it-- it- it was transporting tar. So we had to make-- we had to push ourselves in that boat, on top of that tar, whatever it is. And they took us. Where, we didn't know. Finally a couple days later-- still, they didn't give us any food or water in that-- in that-- for that boat. Finally, after a couple days, we came to a city. I think it was Linz, in Austria. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They took us out. We went out from the boat, five in a line. And we started to march. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Who-- whoever couldn't march, whoever-- whoever got sick or couldn't-- or couldn't march or got slow, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] they shot him, right there. The rest of us had to march again. And so till-- till we came to Ebensee. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] In Ebensee, itself, I didn't work, because it was already-- they realized already, the Germans realized already that the war is-- is close to an end. In Ebensee, we didn't get-- we didn't receive almost nothing to eat. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] The people there, they were dying on the-- on the street, in the barrack. It was-- it was something, you know, undescribable. And they just took them, as the person-- as the person died, they just took him on a wagon and out. I think-- I think in the whole time I was in Ebensee, they fed us once. And what that feeding was, I-- it was a soup. I mean it was hot water with potato peels. You were lucky if you got the potato peels. I think that was once or twice the most in there. Till finally, May 7, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the Americans came in. But before the Americans came in, there was two days where there was actually nobody there, no authority there. There was only the German civilians outside, no SS or anything. It was free-for-all in Ebensee. Whoever-- whoever had something-- something to say about a kapo-- and most of them were bad-- they never got out alive there. I have seen, myself, where they killed a Polish kapo. Then they got a hold on that kapo, what I mentioned before, they got a hold of him. But he-- I was outside of the barrack at that time. For some reason or another, he tore himself out from there and he ran out. He jumped out-- he jumped out-- out of the-- of the barbed wire. And there was that-- the German guard, that civilian guard. That civilian guard told him, halt. And this kapo hollered, ich-- ich bin ein Deutscher. So the German guard told him, macht schnell. That was the last I saw of him, of that guard. Anyway, the Americans came in, over there. I don't think that they knew what they were doing either. They didn't realize what-- what they are dealing with, who they are dealing with. They started to give food. Whoever could go for that food, they got sick right away afterwards. Then the next day, they took some town people. They took all those dead bodies. The town people had to take all those dead bodies and put it on wagons and take it out somewhere to burn them or to bury them, whatever it was. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then, a few days later, everybody had to register where he's from. Of course, I registered I'm from Czechoslovakia. It was a rainy day. It came a-- a truck, an open truck. They took us in the open truck, whoever was going to Czechoslovakia. They put us in the open truck, with the rain and everything. I don't remember that they even gave us any provisions, on the way, till we came to Czechoslovakia. But Czechoslovakia, they dropped us off. I think it was a monastery, because I've seen nuns over there. On my luck, I came right after they finished lunch. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I came over there, I could hardly walk. I was sick. I came over there, and the nun says to me, we don't have no more food. I'm sorry, you came after lunch. But I think I got a-- something. She came out and gave me something to eat. I've seen that I don't have anybody here. I don't know the city. I don't know nobody. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I went to the station, to the railroad station. Of course, I don't have no money. I'll be honest. I begged for food. Came to the station, I took the train. I knew I have to go east to my hometown. I came to a city in Slovakia.

speaker: interviewee
text: The name? What was the name? Bratislava. I think it calls, what, Pressburg in Yiddish. I came there. I found over there people from our town in Bratislava.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he told me-- that was the person what told me about my brother, that my brother is alive. This is how I knew that my brother is alive. From Bratislava I made myself, little by little, to Budapest. And from Budapest, I made myself all the way up to our town. Of course, sometimes, I had to ride on top of the train, because there was no room inside. There was no such a thing as buying tickets or anything. Just, you go on the train and-- and you ride, till I came to our town. In our town, I found already a few-- a few people, young people, from our town. After that-- after that, a cousin of mine, from our-- what was there already. He told me-- I think a couple months later, he told me, Herschey, I thought that you were going to be the first one that we were going to have to bury in our cemetery, the way you looked-- the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was in our town. I was still the high holidays. It was in 1945. I've seen there is no future there. The reason for it was plain. We found out it was occupied by Russia.

speaker: interviewee
text: I found out that the Russians want to take us-- we are young-- they want to take us in the army. I said, no. I'm not going to go in the army, not for Russia, not for anybody. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So me and a couple other young people went ahead. We knew that there is illegal immigration to-- well, to Palestine. They called Israel, at that time, Palestine.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the only way to reach that destination is we had to go Budapest. We came to Budapest also without any money, without anything. But before-- excuse me-- before we came to Budapest, we were at a station in Teresva. We had to wait for the train, me and that young girl. She was, at that time, I would say about 16 years old. I went away for a couple seconds from the station. All of a sudden, I hear her screaming. I run back. And I see there are two Russian soldiers are molesting her. That was in the nighttime. I ran over and I told them, in Russian, [RUSSIAN], that is my wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: He looked at me. Like this, he says to me, [RUSSIAN]? I said, da. And he let her go.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So anyway, we made the train. We came to Budapest. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In Budapest, the-- the Jewish agency, actually, the Zionist agency, took it over from there. They gave us food. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: It was only-- not me alone. There was quite a few people, all young people. They told us that in a couple days, we are going to go to Austria. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But we are going to have to go from Hungary to Austria at night. In other words, because we were illegal. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] They told us, in case of we-- if their-- if they catch us, by the-- by the Austrian border, we should say that we are Greeks. We were in the concentration camp in the east. We are going home to Greece. That, we have to say. They didn't give us any papers. But that's what we have to say. We are Greeks. We are going there. At night, they took us from Budapest on a train toward the next-- to the last stop, the Austria-Hungarian border. Down there, they took us down with two civilians. Two civilians were waiting for us. They knew how to go over the border. They took us over the border. And we came to the Austrian-- they have a railroad station there. There, somebody was waiting. And he told us, we should take the train. And we should take the train to Judenburg, in Austria. We are-- we are all Greek immigrants. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We took the train to Judenburg. We were there a day or maybe a day and a half, something like it. From there, they took us to a city named Villach. That's also in Austria, near the Italian border. Now, all that was occupied by the British, because Austria was occupied by-- by the four-- by the four powers, the United States, Russia, France, and-- and the British. That part was occupied by the British. Of course, the British didn't want anybody to go to Palestine at that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: When we came to Villach, my name had to be changed. They changed my name to Modrich Nuchem. Why? Because the-- the people of that camp changed all the time. But the name-- the names remained.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the people that were in that camp, they used to ship them, illegal, to-- to Italy. And new ones came in. But they used the same name. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We're there a few days. They told us, tonight, you're going to go, in boxcars, over this-- over the-- over the border to Italy. Nobody should talk, say a word. Nobody should cough. Of course, we were all young at that time. The-- I think oldest one was about 28, 30 years old. Because the elderly people, they-- Hitler took care on them.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we came in the-- in the box-- we went in the boxcars. We sat there. And he says, it's only going to be two stations what you're going to go through. The third station's already Italy.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, he says, in case, if the British are going to stop you, don't say a word. Even if they talk to you English, you don't know, of course. Don't answer. They talk to you German, don't answer. Don't answer a word. If they stop you, he says, they're going to bring you back here in the camp. But before, before the-- the train stops for the station to bring you back, when the train slows down, jump out of the cars, out of the train cars, and run straight to the camp. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Of course, it happened all of a sudden. By the second station, the English opened up, the British opened up the cars. And there we are.

speaker: interviewee
text: They started to ask, what is your name? What is dein name? Nobody says a word.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then they take and they close the cars. They didn't close it, basically. They didn't close the doors. They left the doors open. The train starts to go back. As soon as the train slowed down by Villach, it slowed down already, we started to jump. We jumped out of that car-- those cars. And we ran straight to the station-- to the-- to the camp. We came to the camp. We mingled, right away, with the rest of the people. The British, they came back in the camp. They ask who was on that train. Of course, nobody was on that train. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So they liquidated our camp, right away there. They realized from where the people are going. They took us, in a camp, by the name Trofaiach, in Austria. I was there, in the winter, from 1940-- let's see, in the-- the winter of 1945 I was in Trofaiach.

speaker: interviewee
text: From Trofaiach, they liquidated that camp, because we were only Jewish people in that camp. But they-- then, they made that camp for a lot of-- for a lot of-- there were Poles, Russians, and so on, displaced person. They made that camp for them. They took us to another camp by the name of Koblenz-- no, Kobenz. From Kobenz, we were there just about three months. From Kobenz, they took us to Admont. In 1946, they took us to Admont. I was in Admont till-- to-- till 1949. Yeah, in the spring of 1949, I was-- I was in Admont. And Admont, basically, also was a camp. There's a lot of people left for the-- for Israel. I was working in Admont not for the British. I-- I say the truth. I have I did a lot for the people what wanted to go to Israel. I made a lot of arrangements for them, with a-- with a Jewish agency, they should be able to go to Israel. I changed-- no. My brother changed my mind. I wanted to go to Israel, too. My brother, he was in Israel. He changed my mind. That was in 1948, when the war started there. He says to me, he wrote me, that, he says, don't come now to Israel. He says, It's a war. He says, go to the United States. You'll be alive there. Now, there-- there is something that I wanted to say how I-- I had an uncle here-- how I found my uncle. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That was in 1946. I was reading the Jewish-- the Jewish paper, They used to send the-- the paper to the camp. And, of course, there was the classified ad.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was people, you know, they were looking for their relatives. Who-- who is alive? And I've seen, over there, a name from next to our town, that he's looking for some relatives. I figure maybe he knows my uncle.

speaker: interviewee
text: I did-- I did write to him that this and this is my uncle's name, Harry Fox. He was in-- he was in Europe. He came for a visit in 1940. Maybe you know him. So I would like, you know, you should-- you should tell him that I'm alive and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, of course, he notified my uncle. He knew my uncle. He notified him. This is how-- this is how I-- I-- I got in touch with my uncle. And he's the one what did send me the address of my brother, because he knew my brother is in Israel. They were corresponding, already, with each other. So I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my brother send-- my uncle send me papers, I should be able to come to the United States. Because, at that time, he had the guarantee that I'm not going to be a burden to the-- to the government. He signed the papers for me. And I came here in November 7, 1949.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When I came down from the ship, I don't think the United States made a very good impression on me for a simple reason. It November 7. It was very-- it was-- it was the-- the weather was very bad. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My aunt took me from the ship back to Brooklyn. She used to live in Brighton. And now I know where it-- how we went. She took a taxi. We went through the garment district. It was exactly around lunchtime. And there I see there's a red light. And people are walking against the red, with the red. I didn't see that before. I know I was in Austria. Down there, it was-- it was, if the-- if the light is red, you wait. If the tramway is full, you wait for the next tramway. This made of me such a bad impression. And besides, I didn't realize that they had election a day before, the 6th. All the papers, you know the advertisements, what they threw, for the candidates was all on the floor. [LAUGHS] I didn't see that in Austria. You know, so it basically, it made a very bad impression on me. But thanks, God, I'm very glad I came here. That was just an impression, a bad impression. But basically life, life wasn't-- wasn't that bad here.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK We're-- we're going to stop now and change tapes.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you.
